title: Biodiversity and conservation
examTip: Biodiversity questions are often evaluation questions — why is biodiversity important, what threatens it, how do we conserve it? Know specific conservation methods (seed banks, captive breeding, protected areas, international agreements) and be ready to evaluate each. AQA also tests the difference between species biodiversity and genetic biodiversity.
introTips: Biodiversity isn't just about having lots of different animals — it's about the health and resilience of entire ecosystems.
AQA often asks why biodiversity matters. Think about ecosystem stability, medicines from nature, food security, and ecosystem services like pollination.
Conservation methods come up in 6-mark questions — you need pros and cons of each, not just a list.
intro: Biodiversity — the variety of life on Earth — has been declining rapidly due to human activity. We've already lost thousands of species, and many more are threatened. Why does this matter? Because every species plays a role in its ecosystem, and ecosystems provide services that humans depend on. Understanding threats and conservation methods is increasingly important both scientifically and politically.

keyPoints.heading: What biodiversity means and why it matters
keyPoints.content: Biodiversity refers to the variety of different species of organisms on Earth, or within a particular ecosystem. There are two components: species biodiversity (the number of different species in an area) and genetic biodiversity (the variety of alleles within a species). Biodiversity matters because: (1) stable ecosystems — more species = more complex food webs = greater resilience to change; (2) ecosystem services — pollination, water purification, soil fertility, oxygen production; (3) medicines — many drugs come from wild plants and organisms; (4) food security — genetic diversity in crops allows breeding for disease resistance; (5) moral argument — species have intrinsic value and the right to exist.
writeBullets: Biodiversity = variety of different species in an area OR variety of alleles within a species
High biodiversity = more stable ecosystem (complex food webs, more resilient to change)
Ecosystem services: pollination, water purification, soil fertility, oxygen production
Medicines: many drugs derived from wild plant/animal species
Food security: genetic diversity allows crop breeding for disease resistance
Moral argument: species have intrinsic value regardless of usefulness to humans

keyTerms.def: The variety of life in an area — includes species biodiversity (number of different species) and genetic biodiversity (variety of alleles within a species)
The number and variety of different species living in a given area
The variety of alleles (forms of genes) found within a population or species
The benefits that healthy ecosystems provide to humans, including pollination, water purification, soil fertility, and climate regulation

keyPoints.memTip: Why does biodiversity matter? SEMM: Stability (more resilient ecosystems), Ecosystem services (pollination, clean water), Medicines (drugs from nature), Moral (right to exist). Give all four in an extended answer.

keyPoints.heading: Threats to biodiversity
keyPoints.content: Human activities are the primary threat to biodiversity. Key threats include: Habitat destruction — deforestation, draining wetlands, urban development — the largest driver of species extinction; Climate change — changing temperatures and rainfall patterns shift where species can survive; Pollution — pesticides, plastic, oil spills, water pollution harm organisms directly; Invasive species — non-native species introduced by humans can outcompete or prey on native species; Over-exploitation — overfishing, overhunting reducing populations below viable levels. The rate of species extinction is now estimated to be 100–1,000 times higher than the natural 'background' rate.
writeBullets: Habitat destruction: largest driver — deforestation, wetland draining, urban development
Climate change: shifts temperature/rainfall → species can't survive in current ranges
Pollution: pesticides, plastics, oil spills harm organisms
Invasive species: outcompete native species, disrupt ecosystems
Over-exploitation: overfishing, overhunting → populations below viable levels
Extinction rate now 100–1,000× natural background rate

keyTerms.def: The removal or degradation of natural habitats, the leading cause of biodiversity loss worldwide
A non-native species introduced to an area (accidentally or deliberately) that outcompetes native species, disrupting the ecosystem
Harvesting species (through fishing, hunting, collection) faster than populations can recover

keyPoints.memTip: Five threats: HCPIO — Habitat destruction, Climate change, Pollution, Invasive species, Over-exploitation. Habitat destruction is always the biggest one to mention first.

keyPoints.heading: Conservation methods
keyPoints.content: Several methods are used to protect biodiversity. Seed banks: seeds from a wide variety of plant species are collected and stored — cheap, long-term insurance against extinction, but only works for plants. Captive breeding programmes: endangered animals bred in zoos or wildlife centres, then reintroduced to the wild — effective but expensive, and captive animals may struggle to survive in the wild. Protected areas (nature reserves, national parks): areas set aside where human disturbance is restricted — preserves habitat, but animals can still leave, and local human communities may be disadvantaged. International agreements: e.g. CITES (restricts trade in endangered species), Paris Agreement (reducing climate change) — require global cooperation which can be difficult to enforce. Reforestation: planting trees to restore lost habitats.
writeBullets: Seed banks: store seeds of many plant species — cheap, long-term, but only for plants
Captive breeding: breed endangered animals in zoos, reintroduce to wild — effective but expensive
Protected areas: restrict human activity in key habitats — preserves habitat but animals can leave
International agreements (CITES): restrict trade in endangered species — requires global cooperation
Reforestation: restores lost habitat — takes decades, may not recreate original ecosystem

keyTerms.def: A facility that stores seeds from diverse plant species at low temperature to preserve genetic diversity
Breeding endangered animals in controlled environments (zoos) with the aim of reintroducing them to the wild
Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species — an international agreement restricting trade in threatened species

keyPoints.memTip: Four main conservation methods: Seed banks (plants), Captive breeding (animals), Protected areas (habitat), International agreements (global). For each, remember: what it does + one advantage + one limitation. That's the structure AQA wants for evaluation.

tables.title: Conservation methods — advantages and limitations
rows: Only for plants; seeds may lose viability over time
Expensive; animals may not survive reintroduction
Animals can leave; may restrict local livelihoods
Addresses global threats like climate change/trade
Difficult to enforce; requires global cooperation

0.question: Which of the following best explains why high biodiversity makes an ecosystem more stable?
options: More species means more competition, which makes all species stronger
A greater variety of species creates more complex food webs, so if one species is lost, others can fill the same role
High biodiversity prevents any diseases from spreading between organisms
More species means more photosynthesis, which increases oxygen production
0.correctFeedback: ✅ Exactly right — complex food webs mean that the loss of one species is less likely to cause ecosystem collapse, because other species can take over its role.
0.wrongFeedback: High biodiversity creates more complex food webs with more feeding connections. If one species is lost, other species can use different food sources or fill the same ecological role, preventing the collapse of the ecosystem. This is why a diverse rainforest is much more resilient to disruption than a monoculture wheat field.

1.question: What is the main advantage of seed banks as a conservation method?
options: They allow endangered animals to breed safely
They preserve genetic diversity of plant species cheaply and long-term
They protect entire habitats from human disturbance
They prevent invasive species from entering ecosystems
1.correctFeedback: ✅ Right — seed banks are a cost-effective way to preserve the genetic diversity of plant species for the long term, as insurance against extinction.
1.wrongFeedback: Seed banks preserve the seeds (and therefore the genetic diversity) of plant species at low temperatures — they're a cheap, long-term insurance policy against plant extinction. They don't apply to animals, protect habitats, or address invasive species — those require different conservation approaches.

commonMistakes: Confusing species biodiversity and genetic biodiversity — species biodiversity = number of different species. Genetic biodiversity = variety of alleles within a single species. A monoculture of one wheat variety has low genetic biodiversity even if many individual plants are present.
Only listing conservation methods without evaluating — AQA 'evaluate' questions need advantages AND limitations of each method, plus a conclusion.
Forgetting to mention ecosystem services when explaining why biodiversity matters — don't just say 'more species is better'; explain the specific value: pollination, medicines, water purification.
Saying habitat destruction is the only threat — while it's the biggest, pollution, climate change, invasive species, and over-exploitation all contribute. List at least 2–3 in extended answers.
examTips: For 'why does biodiversity matter?' questions, structure your answer around: ecosystem stability, ecosystem services, medicines, food security, moral argument. Each is a separate mark-worthy point.
For conservation method evaluation, always give both an advantage AND a limitation for each method — then a conclusion about which is most effective or appropriate.
When discussing threats, always try to give a specific example (deforestation of rainforests, introduction of grey squirrels outcompeting red squirrels in the UK).
The moral argument — that species have a right to exist — is often overlooked but is a valid AQA mark point in 'why protect biodiversity?' questions.
revisionCardBullets: Biodiversity = variety of species in an area (species biodiversity) OR alleles within a species (genetic biodiversity)
High biodiversity → stable ecosystems, resilient food webs
Ecosystem services: pollination, water purification, soil fertility, oxygen
Many medicines derived from wild species
Main threats: habitat destruction, climate change, pollution, invasive species, over-exploitation
Habitat destruction = largest driver of biodiversity loss
Seed banks: store plant seeds — cheap, long-term, genetic diversity preserved
Captive breeding: endangered animals bred in zoos for reintroduction
Protected areas: restrict human activity in key habitats
International agreements (e.g. CITES): restrict trade in endangered species
